High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (98.33) and strong privacy/anti-surveillance stance (82.47) support transparency, but very low trust in government (16.53) and institutions tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Very low openness and high conscientiousness plus a dangerous worldview motivate cautious support for transparency as a system-stabilizing reform, but low agreeableness and strong anti-egalitarianism keep enthusiasm from being strong.

High authoritarianism (69.65) and traditionalism (99.63) combined with low anti-elitism (1.75) and low liberty-oppression (1.57) make this person skeptical of forcing transparency on platforms, seeing it as unnecessary interference with established order.

High trust in institutions and moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation produce mild support for transparency, but very low openness and a competitive worldview temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High dutifulness and system justification combine with strong competitive jungle worldview to back clear rules against deceptive actors, while low libertarianism and moderate civic nationalism temper any maximalism.
